settings.sync.info.description.text:
AI Education Weekly can sync your settings and read history across all your devices. This data is stored securely on Kagi servers and associated with your Kagi Search account.

Your synced data is not used for any other purpose, not shared with anyone, and is solely stored to provide the sync service to you. You have full control over what gets synced and can delete your data at any time.

about.approach.description1.text: World doesn't live in echo chambers. The reality emerges from the collision of different viewpoints and perspectives - that's how we separate signal from noise.
about.approach.description1.translationContext: First paragraph of the approach section

about.approach.description2.text: We strive for diversity and transparency of resources and welcome your contributions to widen perspectives. This multi-source approach helps reveal the full picture beyond any single viewpoint.

about.approach.description3.text: AI Education Weekly reads public RSS feeds of thousands of (community-curated) world-wide news sources and utilizes AI to distill them into one perfect daily briefing. You get every critical perspective and timeline in just 5 minutes. That's it. No endless scrolling. No attention hijacking. Because we deserve better.

about.contact.description.text: For feedback, please use Kagi Feedback. For support, support@kagi.com.

contribute.onboarding.community.descript.text: No Kagi staff member decides what categories exist or what stories show up. All of that comes from the feeds, and the feeds come from the community.

contribute.onboarding.community.networkL.translationContext: Label on the story cluster nodes in the feed flow animation
Label on the contributor node in the feed network animation

contribute.onboarding.community.networkN.text: Feeds on the left flow into stories on the right. More feeds means more stories and better coverage.
contribute.onboarding.community.networkN.translationContext: Caption under the feed-to-story flow animation

contribute.onboarding.community.noStorie.text: Sometimes a category doesn't produce any stories. That usually means there aren't enough feeds with overlapping coverage for the grouping to kick in. More feeds means more overlap, which means more stories.
contribute.onboarding.community.noStorie.translationContext: Explaining why some categories don't generate stories

contribute.onboarding.community.title.translationContext: Section title for explaining community-driven nature of categories

contribute.onboarding.community.whyXnotY.text: If you're wondering why there's a category for X but not Y, it's because someone who cared about X took the time to add feeds for it. If a topic you care about is missing, you can be the one to start it.
contribute.onboarding.community.whyXnotY.translationContext: Explaining why certain categories exist and others don't

contribute.onboarding.cta.translationContext: CTA button on the onboarding page to proceed to the contribute form

contribute.onboarding.feedTypes.communit.text: Curated by contributors like you in kite_feeds.json. Cover everything from regional news to niche topics. This is where your contribution goes.
contribute.onboarding.feedTypes.communit.translationContext: Description of community feeds that contributors add to
Title for the community feeds card

contribute.onboarding.feedTypes.core.des.text: Cover essential categories like World News, Business, Technology, Science, Sports, and Culture. They're fully open source on GitHub, so you can contribute to them too.
contribute.onboarding.feedTypes.core.des.translationContext: Description of core feeds, emphasizing they are open source and open to contributions

contribute.onboarding.feedTypes.core.tit.translationContext: Title for the core feeds card

contribute.onboarding.feedTypes.title.text: Two kinds of feeds power AI Education Weekly
contribute.onboarding.feedTypes.title.translationContext: Section title for core vs community feeds comparison

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.anyLang.text: Any language is welcome. AI Education Weekly translates everything automatically, so one category can and should mix languages.
contribute.onboarding.guidelines.anyLang.translationContext: Guideline: feeds in any language are accepted

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.minimum.text: New categories need at least 25 feeds to be surfaced in AI Education Weekly. This ensures enough overlap for clustering to work.
contribute.onboarding.guidelines.minimum.translationContext: Guideline: minimum feed count for new categories

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.noLangu.text: Don't create separate categories by language. Use "Switzerland", not "Switzerland (DE)" and "Switzerland (FR)".
contribute.onboarding.guidelines.noLangu.translationContext: Guideline: don't split categories by language

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.quality.text: Prefer established news sources, blogs, or outlets. More feeds is generally better — the clustering handles the rest.

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.rssOnly.text: RSS or Atom feeds only. AI Education Weekly doesn't scrape websites, it only uses publicly available feed data.
contribute.onboarding.guidelines.rssOnly.translationContext: Guideline: only RSS/Atom feeds are accepted

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.title.translationContext: Section title for feed submission guidelines

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.topicSp.text: Feeds should cover the category topic specifically. Adding a general newspaper like The Guardian to a "UK" category will mostly produce world news clusters, not UK-specific ones. Look for topic-filtered feeds, or use RSS filtering tools to narrow a broad feed down.
contribute.onboarding.guidelines.topicSp.translationContext: Guideline: feeds should be topic-specific, not general outlets, with example

contribute.onboarding.guidelines.working.text: Feeds must be working and have recent (ideally daily) content.

contribute.onboarding.pipeline.note.text: The whole thing runs on autopilot. No one at Kagi picks what stories appear or which categories get created.
contribute.onboarding.pipeline.note.translationContext: Note below the pipeline emphasizing the automated nature

contribute.onboarding.pipeline.step1.des.text: Every category has a list of RSS feeds behind it. The system pulls in new articles from all of them. Some categories have hundreds of feeds, others are just getting started.

contribute.onboarding.pipeline.step2.des.text: The system figures out which articles are about the same thing and groups them together. Each group becomes a "story". If an article doesn't match anything else, it gets skipped.

contribute.onboarding.pipeline.step3.des.text: AI reads all the articles in a group and writes a single summary, pulling in the different angles from each source.

contribute.onboarding.pipeline.title.translationContext: Section title for the pipeline explanation

contribute.onboarding.skip.translationContext: Link to skip the onboarding and go straight to the contribute form

contribute.onboarding.subtitle.text: Help build a better news experience by adding RSS feeds to community-curated categories.
contribute.onboarding.subtitle.translationContext: Subtitle on the contribute onboarding info page

contribute.onboarding.title.translationContext: Title on the contribute onboarding info page

worldTension.consistencyText.text: Between alarming headlines, doomscrolling, and 24/7 news cycles, it can feel like the world is constantly on fire. This index aims to provide a grounded perspective. Often, things aren't as bad as they feel.
worldTension.consistencyText.translationContext: Explanation of purpose: cutting through media noise

worldTension.consistencyTitle.translationContext: Section header explaining purpose of the index

worldTension.cool.translationContext: Temperature description for low world tension (0-20%)

worldTension.critical.translationContext: Status label for 61-80% range (deprecated, use hot)

worldTension.elevated.translationContext: Status label for 21-40% range (deprecated, use mild)

worldTension.explanation1.text: The World Tension index provides a temperature reading of global stability based on current events.
worldTension.explanation1.translationContext: First paragraph of world tension explanation

worldTension.explanation2.text: By analyzing headlines from around the world, we track patterns of conflict, crisis, and instability to create a simple metric that shows how "heated" global affairs are at any given moment.
worldTension.explanation2.translationContext: Second paragraph of world tension explanation

worldTension.explanation3.text: Think of it as a global thermometer - when tensions rise due to conflicts, disasters, or political turmoil, the temperature goes up. During calmer periods of stability and cooperation, it cools down.
worldTension.explanation3.translationContext: Third paragraph of world tension explanation

worldTension.extreme.translationContext: Status label for 81-100% range (deprecated, use burning)

worldTension.factor.novelty.text: Novelty — New crises or escalations are weighted more heavily than ongoing situations that are already "priced in."

worldTension.factor.reversibility.text: Reversibility — Irreversible actions (invasions, loss of life) are weighted more than reversible ones (sanctions, rhetoric).

worldTension.factor.scope.text: Scope of impact — Global systemic risks, economic contagion, and alliance triggers are weighed against localized events.

worldTension.factor.worstCase.text: Proximity to worst case — How close events are to catastrophic outcomes, with nuclear/WMD situations given outsized weight.

worldTension.factorsTitle.text: The AI considers four key factors:

worldTension.high.translationContext: Status label for 41-60% range (deprecated, use warm)

worldTension.hot.translationContext: Temperature description for serious world tension (61-80%)

worldTension.methodologyIntro.text: The World Tension index is generated by AI analysis of current World news headlines. Rather than a fixed formula, it uses reasoning to evaluate global stability.

worldTension.mild.translationContext: Temperature description for normal world tension (21-40%)

worldTension.stable.translationContext: Status label for 0-20% range (deprecated, use cool)

worldTension.title.translationContext: Title for the world tension modal

worldTension.warm.translationContext: Temperature description for elevated world tension (41-60%)
